coredata.dc:title: Unraveling the role of agro waste-derived graphene quantum dots on dielectric and mechanical property of the fly ash based polymer nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Chaturvedi, Ashish Kumar
Pappu, Asokan
Gupta, Manoj Kumar
coredata.dc:description: In this study, crystalline graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is prepared using agro waste of paddy straw via hydrothermal route and GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposites was prepared for the first time. HR-TEM and XRD investigations confirmed the formation of hexagonal GQDs with average diameter of 8 nm. Nanocomposite prepared with GQDs reinforcement in fly ash waste under epoxy system was fabricated through the compressive molding machine. Graphene quantum dots-fly ash polymer nanocomposites showed a substantial and concurrent increase in dielectric constant (ɛ') up to ~ 350 compared to fly ash based polymer composites (ɛ'~13). The GQDs-reinforced fly ash based nanocomposites possess the high flexural strength of 60 MPa, whereas pristine fly ash polymer hybrid composite exhibited flexural strength of 35 MPa. The unconventional dielectric enhancement and flexural strength of agro waste derived GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposite is attributed to the sudden increase in the electric dipoles and improved interfacial and chemical bonding of crystalline GQDs with fly ash and epoxy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paddy straw
GQDs
Composites
Flexural strength
Dielectric properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs from paddy straw

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic presentation of growth of paddy straw derived GQDs and development of GQDs-fly ash nanocomposites through compressive molding technique (b) flow chart for development of GQDs reinforcement of -fly ash nanocomposites.
FT-IR spectrum of pristine fly ash composite and GDQs reinforced fly ash-epoxy nanocomposite.
(a) SEM of ball-milled fly ash waste particulates and (b) GQDs-fly ash waste nanocomposites, (c) HR-TEM image of GQDs (d) HR-TEM lattice fringes image of the GQDs.
XRD spectrum of agrowaste derived graphene quantum dots.

Variation of (a) dielectric constant of fly ash composite (b) GQDs-fly ash nanocomposites (c) dielectric loss of fly ash composite and (d) GQDs-fly ash nanocomposites (e) ac conductivity of fly ash composites and (f) GQDs-fly ash nanocomposites with frequency.
Stress-strain curve (flexural strength) of fly ash polymer composite and GQDs- fly ash nanocomposites.
(a) Low and (b, c) high magnified morphological analysis fracture surface of GQDs-reinforced fly ash nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Unraveling the role of agro waste-derived graphene quantum dots on dielectric and mechanical property of the fly ash based polymer nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR, Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh 462026, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR, Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CSIR- Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute, Madhya Pradesh-462026, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad 201002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad-201002, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: CSIR, Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh 462026, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR, Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, crystalline graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is prepared using agro waste of paddy straw via hydrothermal route and GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposites was prepared for the first time. HR-TEM and XRD investigations confirmed the formation of hexagonal GQDs with average diameter of 8 nm. Nanocomposite prepared with GQDs reinforcement in fly ash waste under epoxy system was fabricated through the compressive molding machine. Graphene quantum dots-fly ash polymer nanocomposites showed a substantial and concurrent increase in dielectric constant (ɛ') up to ~ 350 compared to fly ash based polymer composites (ɛ'~13). The GQDs-reinforced fly ash based nanocomposites possess the high flexural strength of 60 MPa, whereas pristine fly ash polymer hybrid composite exhibited flexural strength of 35 MPa. The unconventional dielectric enhancement and flexural strength of agro waste derived GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposite is attributed to the sudden increase in the electric dipoles and improved interfacial and chemical bonding of crystalline GQDs with fly ash and epoxy.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid increase in the demand for food and electricity due to fast growth in population intensified the industrial and agricultural activities, causing huge generation of organic and inorganic wastes. Among various agro waste, paddy straw is one of the major contributor and annually about 150 million tons of paddy straw are generated worldwide [1,2]. Similarly, with rising demand for electricity, huge raw material in the form of coal supplies is required for thermal power plant that later produced fly ash as waste materials of around 250 MT annually. The wastes generated from agriculture and industrial waste such paddy straw and fly ash waste are an escalating issue and their proper utilization as value added products are urgently needed across the world. To dispose the paddy straw waste, usually, it was used to burn in the open field that produced severe air pollution causes numerous threats to human life/health. Similarly, huge amount of fly ash that content silica, alumina and iron oxides are disposed in the open area surface which results groundwater get contaminated from leaching of hazardous chemicals from fly ash [3]. To address these problem, recently, various researchers have been reported that fly ash may be used as reinforcement materials for cement, bricks and composite materials [4]. However due to brittle nature, low mechanical strength and low dielectric constant of the fly ash composite their potential applications are restricted. Moreover, tuning mechanical and electric property of fly ash based composite system are very challenging and highly desirable for their numerous applications including smart building materials, charge storage, aerospace component, flame retardant, sensor, electronics and self-healing materials [4–8]. To improve the properties of composites, several nanoparticles such carbon nanotubes, graphene and silica nanomaterial were also used. However, growth of these nanomaterials involves sophisticated process and their dispersion in the polymer matrix is very challenging. Graphene nanostructures have received potential attention owing to their intrinsic and outstanding physical, chemical and electronic properties including high mechanical stiffness [9–12]. Particularly, GQDs, a zero dimensional materials possess the intriguing properties of graphene and it also exhibits extraordinary properties such as non-zero band gap, quantum confinement effects and edge effects. However, GQDs, has not been yet utilized to alter the dielectric and mechanical properties of fly ash-polymer nanocomposite. Moreover, the growth of the GQDs from waste materials are hardly reported. Recently, our group has successfully developed the GQDs from the bio waste such as paddy straw and their application in the stone waste was discussed [13]. However, synthesis of the GQDs reinforced-fly ash nanocomposites was not yet reported. The fly ash nanocomposites have several applications in electronic, charge storage device, shape memory application and strain sensor application [8,14]. In present work, the effect of GQDs on the dielectric properties and the mechanical properties (flexural strength) of fly ash nanocomposites were investigated for the first time. We have successfully improved the dielectric constant and mechanical property of fly ash polymer nanocomposite though surface modification of highly dispersed GQDs in fly ash particulates through compressive moulding techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs from paddy straw
ce:section.ce:para: Agro-waste of paddy straw was collected from farmer field and powder of paddy straw was prepared though the high energy ball milling process. The processes straw was mixed with 0.5 M HCL and whole solution was kept in the hydrothermal autoclave. The detailed synthesis process of the quantum dot was reported in our previous work [13]. To prepare the GQDs-fly ash reinforced polymer nanocomposites, initially fly ash was received from Sarni thermal power station, Betul region of Madhya Pradesh state, India. Fly ash was washed with DI water and heated up for 12 h. The fly ash was ball milled to reduce their size using high energy planetary ball mill under 300 rpm for 7 h condition. The GQDs of 0.1 vol% was mixed in the ball milled fly ash powder under DMF solution. To fabricate the GQDs- fly ash nanocomposite, 50% (vol) of the GQDs-fly ash ball milled powder was blended homogenously with epoxy (C21H24O4). The Lapox B-11 was used as hardener (Atul Ltd, India). The solution was poured in the mould and pressure of about 100 Kg/cm2 was applied through the compressive moulding machine. The GQDs-fly ash was obtained after demoulding process at room temperature.

X-Ray diffraction (XRD) technique is employed to measure the crystalline and structural properties of GQDs and fly ash particulates. Functional group present in the GQDs and nanocomposites products were measured by the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy technique under frequency range of 400–4000 cm−1 (Thermo Scientific Nicolet machine no iS50 FT-IR spectrometer). Field emission electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and HR-Transmission electron microscopies were employed to record the morphological images of GQDs and GQDs-reinforced fly ash nanocomposites, respectively. Dielectric property of the samples was investigated by Keysight impedance analyzer (E4980A). Universal testing machine (Ametek, Bongos Regis, Lloyd Instruments, UK) was used to examine the flexural strength of the nanocomposites as per ASTM D790–17.

The synthesis process of GQDs from agro -waste (paddy straw) and fabrication of the nanocomposite prepared from GQDs and fly ash are presented in the Fig. 1a. The paddy straw derived GQDs exhibited the characteristic UV light under wavelength of the 254 nm illumination. Lower side of the Fig. 1 shows the actual photograph of the GQDs-reinforced fly ash nanocomposite sample. The flow chart of the synthesis and fabrication of GQDs-reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposite is show in the Fig. 1b.

The FT-IR spectra of ball milled pristine fly ash composite and GQDs-fly ash polymer nanocomposite structures were recorded in wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1 at room temperature ( Fig. 2). The FT-IR spectra fly ash composite and GQDs reinforced fly ash nanocomposite exhibits different absorption bands in different frequencies regions. In fly ash hybrid composite, the absorption bands are detected at 796, 1054,1245, 1510,1630, 2920, 2961 and 3370 cmˉ1. However, GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposite exhibits the band at 770, 1031, 1243, 1507, 1636, 2925, 2962, 3032 and 3373 cmˉ1. The peak appeared at 770 cm−1 suggests the asymmetric stretching of epoxy. The absorption band occurs at 1636 and 1630 cm−1 are due to Si-O-Si bonds of quartz (SiO2). An absorption band at 796 cm−1 arises due to the symmetric stretching of the Al-O-Si and Si-O-Si bonds of fly ash. The FT-IR peak detected at 3370 cmˉ1 depicts the O-H stretching of graphene quantum dots (Fig. 2). The peaks at 1245 cm−1 is due to the functional group of C–H and peak at 1031 cm−1 indicates the existence of and C–O. The peaks observed at 1508 cm−1 is the characteristic peaks of the GQDs which attributed to stretching vibrations of CC in aromatic groups.

Fig. 3 showed the field emission-scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) images of ball milled fly ash particulates and graphene quantum dots-reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposites. Fig. 3(a) shows the FE-SEM image of high energy ball-milled fly ash waste powder in which the various irregular shapes morphology of circular with size of 200 nm–2.00 µm can be seen. Fig. 3(b) represent the FE-SEM images of GQDs-reinforced fly ash nanocomposites. The FE-SEM image shows that GQDs are distributed within the matrix and a strong interfacial interaction between GQDs, epoxy and fly ash is formed. HR-TEM image revels that GQDs has circular morphology with average diameter of 8 nm (Fig. 3(c)). The high resolution lattice image fringes is also recorded and presented in Fig. 3d. The result indicates high crystallinity of the agro waste derived GQDs, with a lattice parameter of 0.24 nm, corresponds to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene.

The X-ray diffraction spectrum (XRD) of agro-waste derived GQDs is also recorded. A crystalline and characteristic XRD peak of GQDs at two theta of 25.31◦ was clearly detected which is corresponds to the (002) plane of GQDs as shown in Fig. 4.

The variation of frequency with dielectric constant and dielectric tangent loss of the pristine composite of fly ash and crystalline GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposite was presented in Fig. 5. The dielectric constant (ε′) of fly ash epoxy hybrid composite and GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposites is calculated using given formula [11,12].
(1)
ε
′
=
t ×
C
P
ε
0
× A
Where ε′ is the real part of the dielectric constant, ε0, Cp, A, t are the dielectric constant of free space, capacitance, area and thickness of the samples, respectively. Fig. 5a and b show the variation of dielectric constant (ε′) with frequency for polymer fly ash hybrid composite samples and GQDs reinforced polymer nanocomposite sample, respectively. The dielectric constant decreased with the increase in the frequency. This is due to the overall contribution of dielectric polarization including electronic, dipolar, ionic polarization and space charge polarizations. Pristine fly ash hybrid composites exhibited a dielectric constant of about 10. It is interesting to note that, crystalline GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposite exhibits a high dielectric constant of about 350 at the same frequency. The rapid increase in the dielectric constant is ascribed to the formation of large electric dipoles per unit volume due to nanoscale size of the fly ash and GQDs particles and motion of free charge carriers due to the formation of conductive pathway in the GQDs-fly ash and epoxy polymer. The high value of dielectric constant may be also due the accumulation of charge at the grain boundary created by GQDs, which led to higher value of the dielectric constant.

Dielectric loss (tanδ) of the prepared GQDs reinforced -fly ash polymer nanocomposite were also investigated (Fig. 5c). Very low dissipation factor of 0.15 for pristine fly ash hybrid composite was obtained at high frequency side, however GQDs reinforced fly ash nanocomposite shows the dissipation factor (tanδ) of 7 at high frequency side as presented in Fig. 5(c) and (d). The increase in the dielectric loss is due the heat dissipation from movement of electric dipoles by the conductive GQDs under applied electric filed. The ac conductivity (σ) of nanocomposites were also investigated using the measured value of ϵ and tanδ at various frequency. The ac conductivity ( σ ) of fly ash waste-epoxy composite and GQDs reinforced -fly ash based nanocomposites is calculated using following equations [13,15,16].
(2)
σ = 2 π f tan
δ ϵ r
ε
0
Where, f is applied frequency. It was observed that ac conductivity of both the polymer composites samples (pristine fly ash composites and GQDs reinforced fly ash nanocomposites) increased gradually with increase in applied frequency and it follows the well-known Jonscher power-law relation.
(3)
σ = A
ω
s
Where A and s are constant and exponent s (<1) which is frequency (ω)-dependent parameter, respectively. The GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposites exhibited high ac conductivity of (~ 9.12 ×10 −5 Ω−1m−1) compared to pristine fly ash nanocomposite (~ 9.02 10−6 Ω−1m−1) at the same frequency of 3 KHz (Fig. 5e and f). The increase ac conductivity of crystalline GQDs-fly ash nanocomposites can be explained based on the higher conductivity of crystalline GQDs compared to fly ash and epoxy resin. The pumping force of the applied frequency leads to increase in the ac conductivity which help the transporting the electric charge carriers between the different localized states of fly ash-epoxy matrix. This enabling the movement of trapped charges from the various trapping centres in the nanocomposite.

It is reported that carbon nanostructures are used to increase the flexural strength of the polymer nanocomposites and alloys [15], however, the GQDs derived from the paddy straw are not used to enhance the flexural/mechanical strength of fly ash-epoxy polymer composites yet. We have performed flexural strength measurement of pristine fly ash composites and GQDs reinforced -fly ash polymer nanocomposite as per ASTM standard in three point bending test method. Load was applied at the center of the specimen with a cross head speed of 5 mm/min and a span length of 56.00 mm. The stress-strain graph of pristine fly ash composite and GQDs-reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposite are given in Fig. 6. Pristine fly ash-epoxy composite exhibits the flexural strength of 35 MPa, however GQDs-fly ash nanocomposite shows a high strength of 60 MPa. The dramatic increase in the mechanical strength is cause by the network bonding of GQDs with fly ash particulates and epoxy resin with strong interfacial bonding. To further investigate the interfacial bonding and morphological characteristics of the GQDs-reinforced fly ash nanocomposite, cross-sectional FE-SEM image of fracture nanocomposite sample was recorded ( Fig. 7). Low magnified cross-sectional FE-SEM confirmed that no significant voids or cavity are formed in the GQDs reinforced fly ash nanocomposites, which further confirmed that paddy straw derived GQDs have properly dispersed in the matrix due to the use of polar DMF solvent which results strong interfacial bonding with fly ash was formed. Moreover, high magnified cross-sectional FE-SEM image shows the closer view of distribution and interfacial bonding of GQDs in fly ash-epoxy matrix (Fig. 7b,c). It was proposed that the presence of oxygen and aromatic rings of functional GQDs help strong bonding with epoxy-fly ash system and the presence of functional group of epoxy resin also help GQDs to bond directly with the curing agent, resulting mechanical strength increased [9,16]. The bonding between fly ash and epoxy matrix and the basal planes of agrowaste derived GQDs also strengthens the polymer nanocomposite by hydrogen bonds, van der Waals bonds between the carbonyl groups [17]. In addition, the improved mechanical strength of the GQDs-fly ash nanocomposites is due to extraordinary mechanical properties of GQDs due to the presence of sp2 hybridized covalent bond as compared to fly ash-epoxy composites.

In summary, paddy straw derived crystalline GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposite was developed using the compressive molding techniques for the first time. X-Ray diffraction analysis and HR-TEM image confirmed the formation of crystalline GQDs from the paddy straw. Very high dielectric constant up to 350 was observed for the GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposite. Pristine fly ash composite exhibited very low dielectric constant of 10. The flexural strength of the GQDs-fly ash polymer nanocomposite was drastically enhanced by the reinforcement of GQDs. The GQDs reinforced fly ash polymer nanocomposites showed very high flexural strength of 60 MPa, however, pristine fly ash composite exhibits low flexural strength of 35 MPa. Our current finding suggests a unique and environmental friendly approach not only to convert the organic and inorganic waste in the value added materials such as GQDs-fly ash polymer nanocomposite but also provide a method to increase the dielectric and mechanical properties of nanocomposite by addition of GQDs for numerous applications.

Ashish Kumar Chaturvedi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Asokan Pappu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Manoj Kumar Gupta: Conceptualization, Supervision, Data interpretation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. Manoj Kumar Gupta is thankful to CSIR, India for granting MLP -116 and OLP-201 projects. Mr. Ashish is grateful to AICTE, India for his fellowship.
